---
title: "What Makes a Restaurant Click?"
subtitle: "A Data Mining Study on Online Food Delivery"
author: "Charan Malampati, Arjun, Rahul, Sushant | DWDM Course Project"
format:
  html:
    page-layout: article
    theme:
      light: journal
      dark: darkly
    toggle: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-expand: true
    number-sections: true
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  echo: false
---

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The Question Every Restaurant Owner Is Asking

Two restaurants open on the same street, in the same city, selling similar food at similar prices.

One becomes a customer favourite — five-star reviews, consistent orders, loyal repeat customers. The other struggles — low ratings, declining orders, no visibility on the platform.

What separates them?

Is it the cuisine? The pricing? The delivery speed? The location? Or is it something less obvious — a combination of factors that only the data can reveal?

That is the question this study sets out to answer.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Why This Matters

The rise of platforms like Zomato and Swiggy has fundamentally changed how restaurants compete. A restaurant's visibility, reputation, and survival are now shaped by data — ratings, delivery times, customer reviews, and order volumes — all of it logged, ranked, and surfaced to millions of users every day.

This creates both a challenge and an opportunity:

-   **The challenge:** Restaurants have more competition than ever, and a few bad reviews or slow deliveries can push them off the first page of results permanently.
-   **The opportunity:** The same data that creates the problem can also solve it — if analysed correctly.

This project uses data mining to turn that raw operational data into actionable business insight.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The Datasets

We work with two complementary datasets that together capture the full picture of online food delivery:

| Dataset | What It Contains | Business Relevance |
|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Restaurant Data** | Ratings, cost for two, cuisine type, location, menu details, online order availability | Captures what the restaurant offers and how customers perceive it |
| **Delivery Operations Data** | Delivery personnel details, timestamps, weather, traffic, GPS coordinates, vehicle type | Captures how efficiently the restaurant's orders actually reach customers |

Together, these datasets let us connect **what happens inside the restaurant** with **what happens on the road** — and understand how both affect whether a restaurant succeeds.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The Problem We Are Solving

Despite the availability of rich platform data, most restaurants operate on intuition rather than evidence.

> *"Should we lower our price or improve our rating?"* *"Is our delivery time hurting us or is it just the traffic?"* *"Which cuisine type performs best in our city?"*

These are real business questions. Answering them requires more than just looking at averages — it requires data mining: finding patterns, building models, and extracting insight from thousands of records.

The core problem this study addresses:

> **What are the critical factors that determine whether a restaurant thrives or struggles in the online food delivery ecosystem — and can we model them?**

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## What We Do

This study applies a full data mining pipeline across four phases:

| Phase | What We Do | Business Output |
|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Phase 1 — Preprocessing** | Clean both datasets, fix types, handle missing values, engineer features like delivery distance and pickup delay | A trusted, model-ready dataset |
| **Phase 2 — EDA** | Explore distributions, spot patterns, compare segments, visualise relationships | Answers to the "what is happening?" questions |
| **Phase 3 — Modelling** | Build regression and classification models to predict delivery time and restaurant success | Predictive tools the business can use |
| **Phase 4 — Insights** | Translate findings into recommendations for restaurant owners and platform operators | Something a manager can actually act on |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Who This Is For

This study produces insights useful for two audiences:

**Restaurant owners** — understand which factors in your control (pricing, cuisine, service consistency) most strongly drive ratings and order volume.

**Delivery platform operators** — understand which operational factors (traffic management, rider assignment, pickup timing) most directly reduce delivery time and improve customer experience.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The Story That Follows

The three chapters of this project each answer a different part of the central question:

-   **Phase 1** asks: *Can we trust this data?* — and makes it trustworthy.
-   **Phase 2** asks: *What patterns exist?* — and finds them.
-   **Phase 3** asks: *Can we predict outcomes?* — and builds models that do.

By the end, we will have a data-driven answer to the question that opened this study:

> What makes a restaurant click?

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Navigate the Project

-   [Phase 1 — Preprocessing and Feature Engineering](./01_preprocessing.html)
-   [Phase 2 — Exploratory Data Analysis](./02_eda.html)
-   [Phase 3 — Predictive Modelling](./03_modelling.html)
